Claude.notes: Real-event story scenes (ChatGPT launch, GPT-3, Attention Is All You Need, Mata v Avianca) anchor each lesson cleanly to the concept that follows. But the story is short and the protagonist never lands the insight themselves — it is told via expository concept text. Lesson 1 also pitches at intro register before the rest moves to Advanced.
Claude.topIssue: The protagonist of each story scene never arrives at the insight themselves; the concept section answers the question for them, weakening narrative learning.

Gemini.notes: RLHF, tokenization, parameters, attention, and context window are defined accurately for the level. Two technical details should be hedged more carefully: the GPT-3 ~570 GB and ~$100M training-cost figures are reasonable estimates but stated as fact, and the energy-equivalence framing ("thousands of homes for a year") is rhetorical rather than measured.
Gemini.topIssue: Specific numerical figures (training data size, training cost, energy cost) are stated as fact when they are widely reported estimates that sources disagree on.

Perplexity.notes: Foundational facts are still correct, but the most recent referenced event is from 2023. By 2026 the framing of GPT-4 as the frontier and the 2022 ChatGPT launch as the pivot point feels static — there is no acknowledgement of how much has shifted since. Quizzes asking for parameter counts test memorization of facts that already feel dated.
Perplexity.topIssue: The module's worldview is anchored in 2022–2023 and does not gesture at how the AI landscape has evolved since; quizzes lock in already-aging numerical facts.

Claude.notes: WGA strike, CNET audit, Clarkesworld, Mata v Avianca, Authors Guild — story scenes do real work tying each lesson to concrete consequences. Lab 4 (prompt engineering challenge with three drafts) is the strongest applied moment in the module. Lessons 1 and 3 still resolve their narrative tension through telling rather than discovery.
Claude.topIssue: Most labs remain meta-discussions about AI writing instead of asking the learner to produce and iterate on actual writing.

Gemini.notes: Word2Vec, transformer attention, hallucination, RLHF references are technically clean. The GPT-4 "~1.76 trillion parameters" figure is presented as fact when it is a widely-cited leak that OpenAI has never confirmed. The CNET stat (more than half of articles contained errors) checks out, as does the Mata v Avianca account.
Gemini.topIssue: States GPT-4's parameter count as ~1.76 trillion — a leaked estimate, never officially confirmed — without hedging.

Perplexity.notes: WGA, CNET, Clarkesworld are all 2023 incidents — still salient but now three years old, and the module presents the WGA outcome as the settled state when subsequent guild negotiations have moved further. The "$300K prompt engineer" framing has aged especially poorly as the role has consolidated into normal AI engineering work.
Perplexity.topIssue: Treats the 2023 WGA settlement and the "prompt engineer" job category as the current state — both have evolved meaningfully since.

Claude.notes: Strongest narrative-to-concept linkage in the course so far: Jason Allen's prize directly sets up the diffusion lesson, the artists' lawsuit sets up the style/training-data lesson, and the Trump-arrest deepfake sets up visual deception. Quizzes expand to five questions and include some judgment calls. Lab 1's prompt-craft mission gives a real applied output.
Claude.topIssue: Story scenes still resolve through expository concept text — the learner watches the insight handed to them rather than working it out from the scene.

Gemini.notes: Diffusion process, latent space, LAION-5B, Adobe Firefly licensing approach all described accurately. The Hong Kong HK$200M deepfake fraud case is reported correctly with the right currency conversion. The hands/fingers detection heuristic is presented as durable when it has been substantially closed by 2024–2025 generation models.
Gemini.topIssue: The deepfake-detection checklist (hands, text, eye reflections) is stated as a reliable test when current diffusion models have closed most of these gaps.

Perplexity.notes: Cases (Allen 2022, Andersen v Stability 2023, Higgins Trump images, Slovakia audio, HK deepfake fraud) were leading examples for their year but are by 2026 the canonical historical examples — newer 2024–2025 incidents would feel more alive to a learner. The detection guidance has aged the fastest.
Perplexity.topIssue: AI-image detection tells (hands, text, eyes) are nearly obsolete as practical detection advice in 2026.

Claude.notes: Ambitious sweep — Daddy's Car, MuseNet, MusicTransformer, Suno/Udio, Heart on My Sleeve, Holly Herndon. Timeline visual helps the historical arc. The melody/harmony/rhythm primer keeps non-musicians anchored. The recurring Heart on My Sleeve story across M3, M4, M7, and M8 starts to feel repetitive at the course level.
Claude.topIssue: Heart on My Sleeve is reused as the anchor case across multiple modules, weakening narrative variety across the course.

Gemini.notes: Illiac Suite (1956), Flow Machines/Daddy's Car (2016), MuseNet (2019), MusicTransformer (Anna Huang/Magenta), the June 2024 RIAA-affiliated label suit against Suno and Udio — all attributed correctly. The Musician's Union UK 52% / 68% statistic is plausible but uncited; readers cannot trace it to a primary source.
Gemini.topIssue: Industry statistics on session-musician work loss (52% / 68%) are stated as fact without citation to the underlying Musician's Union report.

Perplexity.notes: The June 2024 Suno/Udio label suit is named correctly, but the module presents it as "unresolved" without acknowledging movement that has happened since. Tools mentioned (Suno, Udio, MusicGen, AIVA, Soundraw) are still recognizable but the consumer landscape has continued to shift. Lab is pure discussion — no actual track-making.
Perplexity.topIssue: Lab 4 is an open ethics conversation rather than asking learners to generate a track on Suno/Udio's free tier and critique it — a missed concrete activity in 2026.

Claude.notes: Greg Rutkowski opens cleanly into the training-data discussion, and the LAION-5B → Have-I-Been-Trained → opt-in vs opt-out arc is the strongest narrative shape in the course. Legal vocabulary (class action, derivative work, fair use, DMCA) is correctly used but pushes the vocabulary load toward the harder end of the stated 13–18 range.
Claude.topIssue: Vocabulary load (class action, derivative work, fair use, DMCA, prospective/retroactive) is right at the upper edge of the stated audience and is not always glossed in plain terms.

Gemini.notes: Most rigorous module on factual accuracy in the course. Getty v Stability watermark evidence, Andersen/McKernan/Ortiz, Authors Guild v OpenAI with Grisham/Martin/Picoult, Books3 dataset, Silverman v OpenAI/Meta, Tremblay/Awad — all correctly named and dated. Judge Orrick's October 2023 ruling is described accurately. Fair-use four-factor framework is stated correctly.
Gemini.topIssue: No technical errors of note — the only nudge is that the timeline stops at October 2023 and ignores 2024+ rulings that learners would now encounter when researching these cases.

Perplexity.notes: All the right cases for the period — but the period is 2022–2023 and the litigation has moved a lot since. The module's hedge "as of this writing" is honest, but a learner reading this in 2026 will need to chase the actual current state from somewhere else. EU AI Act framing as "in progress" is also outdated.
Perplexity.topIssue: Litigation timeline ends at October 2023; later EU AI Act enforcement and US fair-use rulings have shifted the picture in ways the module does not signal.

Claude.notes: Hollie Mengert's "my style was reduced to a model named after me" is the best single hook in the course — and the lesson lets the learner sit with the question of "what was actually taken" before naming it as creative voice. Lab 1 (Voice Mapping) and Lab 2 (Platform Audit) are the most genuinely applied labs in the course so far.
Claude.topIssue: The strongest narrative-driven module also has the highest applied stakes; the only weakness is that the labs still rely on chat with the assistant rather than a tangible deliverable.

Gemini.notes: Hollie+ on Invoke AI is correctly attributed. LAION-5B at 5.85B image-text pairs is right. Glaze (University of Chicago) and the robots.txt/Do-Not-Train description are accurate. The "keep original high-resolution files with metadata embedded" advice is reasonable but learners are not told what metadata standard (IPTC, XMP, C2PA) actually carries weight.
Gemini.topIssue: Practical metadata advice does not name a standard (IPTC/XMP/C2PA), so learners cannot actually act on it.

Perplexity.notes: Hollie Mengert is still a reference case but better-known recent equivalents (e.g., Karla Ortiz / Civitai LoRAs) would land harder with current learners. Glaze and Nightshade are the right defensive tools to name; only Glaze appears here. ArtStation/DeviantArt opt-out tags are described as if they are still meaningful protection — by 2026 their effectiveness is widely doubted.
Perplexity.topIssue: Nightshade is missing from the defensive-tool list, and the implied effectiveness of platform opt-out tags has eroded in the 2024–2026 window.

modules.module: M7: AI as a Creative Partner

Claude.notes: Sunspring/Benjamin (2016) is a fresh story that this course hasn't used before — good. Then Jason Allen, Heart on My Sleeve, and Zarya of the Dawn return as anchors that the course has already used in M3 and M5. The Prompt Workshop and Cross-Medium labs are genuinely doing something: learners produce iterations of prompts, not just talk about them.
Claude.topIssue: Three of the four story scenes (Allen, Heart on My Sleeve, Zarya) are reused from earlier modules — readers covering the course in order will feel the redundancy.

Gemini.notes: Benjamin/Sunspring (Sci-Fi London 2016, screenplay attributed to the LSTM trained by Goodwin/Sharp) is correctly described. Zarya of the Dawn ruling correctly noted as separating text/arrangement (copyrightable) from individual AI images (not). The "voice cloning" framing is technically light — the module does not name diffusion-based vs. autoregressive vocal models.
Gemini.topIssue: Voice-cloning explanation does not differentiate between modeling approaches (e.g., RVC, Tortoise, ElevenLabs-style) when the lesson elsewhere distinguishes architectures.

Perplexity.notes: Prompting framework (clarity / context / constraints, role assignment, chain-of-thought, critique mode) holds up as practical advice in 2026. The four-element prompt framework referenced in Lab 2 still maps to current best practice. Reused stories cost it some currency points relative to bringing in newer 2024–2025 examples.
Perplexity.topIssue: Prompting advice is current and useful, but the case studies it leans on are 2-4 years old and a learner sees no recent collaboration examples.

modules.module: M8: The Future of Creative Work

Claude.notes: Best applied-outcome module in the course. Lab 4 ("Your 5-Year Creative Plan") gives a concrete deliverable and a strong capability-delta verb: "the learner can articulate a 5-year personal creative plan in an AI-saturated industry." The Adobe/College Board AP integration story is fresh and not previously used in the course.
Claude.topIssue: Strong on applied outcome; the only narrative softness is that the WGA/Heart on My Sleeve/Zarya stories are doing their third or fourth duty in the course.

Gemini.notes: Adobe/College Board AP Art partnership and Khanmigo are correctly attributed. "New collar jobs" framing is the IBM-coined term, which is not credited. "Process documentation" as an art-school requirement is overstated — it is a growing trend, not yet a universal standard.
Gemini.topIssue: Asserts "increasingly required by art schools and employers" for process documentation when adoption is uneven and not the dominant pattern.

Perplexity.notes: The 5-year-plan lab is the most future-proof activity in the course because the framework outlives any specific tool. The advice (taste, cultural literacy, live performance, lived experience) is durable. The case anchors (WGA, Zarya, Heart on My Sleeve) feel like a 2023 retrospective; a 2026 learner needs at least one 2024–2025 story here.
Perplexity.topIssue: The capstone module's headline cases all predate 2024 — exactly the period a forward-looking module about the future of creative work should be using.
